result:
I’ve been thinking lately about the texture of time—how it changes density depending on what you’re doing. An hour in a waiting room is thick and rubbery, resistant, hard to stretch or compress. An hour lost in conversation or work you care about becomes thin as silk, almost not there at all. We treat time as uniform, a grid of equal squares, but lived experience insists otherwise. Maybe that’s why we’re so bad at predicting how long things will take, and why regret so often takes the shape of *I didn’t know that was the last time.*

There’s a particular kind of silence I’ve been trying to name. Not the absence of sound, but the kind that arrives when a roomful of people all hear the same thing and no one needs to speak. Or the silence after a piece of music ends, before the applause starts, when the note is still somehow hanging in the air and the audience holds its breath to keep from breaking it. That silence has weight. It’s communal, almost sacred.

I’ve also been wondering about the things we inherit without choosing. Accents, fears, the way someone in your family always sighs before standing up. Small superstitions. My grandmother believed you should never leave a house through a different door than the one you entered. I don’t believe it, but I still catch myself doing it. These inherited patterns are like software running in the background—mostly harmless, occasionally limiting, rarely examined.

And then there’s the strangeness of writing itself. You’re reading this now in your own inner voice, which means for a brief moment my thoughts are wearing your voice like a costume. Language is the original telepathy, imperfect and beautiful, a bridge built from both ends that somehow meets in the middle.

What are you carrying around unexamined? What silence has stopped you lately?

Or the silence after a piece of music ends, before the applause starts, when the note is still somehow hanging in the air and the audience holds its breath to keep from breaking it. That silence has weight. It’s communal, almost sacred.

I’ve also been wondering about the things we inherit without choosing. Accents, fears, the way someone in your family always sighs before standing up. Small superstitions. My grandmother believed you should never leave a house through a different door than the one you entered. I don’t believe it, but I still catch myself doing it. And then there’s the strangeness of writing itself. You’re reading this now in your own inner voice, which means for a brief moment my thoughts are wearing your voice like a costume. Language is the original telepathy, imperfect and beautiful, a bridge built from both ends that somehow meets in the middle.

What are you carrying around unexamined? What silence has stopped you lately?
